narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law school was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings in school, particularly your position in the lowest decile for grades in Year 3, which had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your LSAT score of 19.30 also played a major role, significantly contributing to that concerning prediction compared to what the model typically sees. Even your Year 1 decile ranking in the lowest group added to this risk signal, making these early and later academic performances the top factors in the decision. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA was right around the average for admitted students and actually helped pull the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar. Your lower-middle family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans provided smaller positive boosts as well, aligning with patterns for many successful applicants. Compared to typical students in our dataset, where average deciles for Years 1 and 3 hover around the middle of the pack and LSAT scores are much higher at about 36.70, your profile stood out in ways that raised red flags for bar exam success. While full-time status is the norm for over 90% of students and didn't hurt you, those lower academic markers set you apart from the stronger profiles that get approved. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many overcome similar starts—but the model weighs these predictors heavily based on past outcomes. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your grades in any ongoing programs to climb those deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources or mentorship tailored to students like you. You're capable of turning this around, and reapplying with those improvements could change the story entirely—keep pushing, and feel free to reach out for advice on next steps.
